coredata.dc:title: Electron transfer behaviour of green synthesized carbon quantum dot sensor towards VOC and heavy metal ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraju, Nithya
Selvaraj, Senthilnathan
Singhal, Neeraj
Mohan, Vigneshwaran
Sivalingam, Yuvaraj
Rajaram, Kaushik
Venugopal, Gunasekaran
coredata.dc:description: Present day world, the leakage of volatile organic compound (VOC) such as ethanol in industries, as well as presence of heavy metal ions in drinking water pose a serious threat to human health. This research develops a quantum dot sensor (nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (n-CDs)) for multifaceted applications. n-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal from Citrus medica fruit and analysed using XPS, HRTEM, Zeta-potential, PL, and EDS. The study is unique, which employs the Scanning Kelvin Probe microscope (SKP) technique to investigate VOC sensing by n-CDs with contact potential difference mapping for ethanol gas sensing. n-CDs were first-ever characterized by SKP to resolute work function, and VOC interactions were examined. Energy level diagrams were used to study electron transitions. The luminescence quenching caused by electron transfer from n-CDs to receptors was manipulated to detect Fe3+ metal ions with a detection limit of 1.2 µM, which is well below the WHO’s acceptable limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Citrus Medica
Gas sensing
Fe3+ ion sensing
Hydrothermal method

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very thankful to the Central University of Tamil Nadu, India for providing an infrastructural, institutional; research facility for this project and A special thanks to Mr. Nashath, Research scholar (Department of Microbiology, Central University of Tamil Nadu), who assisted with the biocompatibility test with RBC cells. The datasets generated during and/or analysed in the current study, are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra and corresponding Tauc plot (Inset) of (a) CDs and (b) n-CDs @ various pH (c) n-CDs @ various temperatures (d) n-CDs @ different timings.
Fluorescence Emission Spectra of n-CDs (a) Time-dependent (b) Temperature-dependent (c) pH-dependent (d) Excited at different wavelengths.
XPS spectrum of n-CDs(a), Deconvoluted C 1s spectrum (b), N 1s spectrum (c), and O 1s spectrum(d).
HR-TEM images with the different magnification of synthesized n-CDs (a,b) (Inset: histogram of the particle size), lattice fringes(c,d), SAED pattern (e), diffraction image (f).
(a) Scanning Kelvin Probe raster scan mapping of n-CDs in air and ethanol gas medium, (b). Schematic illustration of Fermi energy (EF) level in air and ethanol environment.
Cyclic voltammetry of n- CDS and Schematic illustration of Fluroscence quenching process of the n- CDs by Fe3+ through induced electron transfer.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of n-CDs coordinated with different metal ions. (b) Intensity Ratio (F0–F)/F0 of n-CDs in the presence of various metal ions. The emission spectra of n-CDs (c) with (0–20 μM) Fe3+ concentration, (d) Fe3+ concentrations (0–2 mM).

(a) The correlationship of fluorescence intensity with the concentration of metal ions (0 to 2 mM). Inset is a Stern Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of n-CDs by the addition of Fe3+ from (0 to 2) mM. (b) The kinetic plot of fluorescence quenching of n-CDs with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 to 20 μM. (c) The photographic image of quenching at different Fe3+ concentrations under UV Cabinet (λ = 365 nm).
(a) Under ambient conditions, (b) under UV light at 365 nm (c) Photographic image of fluorescent ink application.
Comparison of Carbon quantum dots with other quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Electron transfer behaviour of green synthesized carbon quantum dot sensor towards VOC and heavy metal ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, SSN College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, SSN College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai - 603 110 Tamil Nadu

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Present day world, the leakage of volatile organic compound (VOC) such as ethanol in industries, as well as presence of heavy metal ions in drinking water pose a serious threat to human health. This research develops a quantum dot sensor (nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (n-CDs)) for multifaceted applications. n-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal from Citrus medica fruit and analysed using XPS, HRTEM, Zeta-potential, PL, and EDS. The study is unique, which employs the Scanning Kelvin Probe microscope (SKP) technique to investigate VOC sensing by n-CDs with contact potential difference mapping for ethanol gas sensing. n-CDs were first-ever characterized by SKP to resolute work function, and VOC interactions were examined. Energy level diagrams were used to study electron transitions. The luminescence quenching caused by electron transfer from n-CDs to receptors was manipulated to detect Fe3+ metal ions with a detection limit of 1.2 µM, which is well below the WHO’s acceptable limit.

ce:section.ce:para: Research studies seek to develop cost-effective analytical techniques that are easy, innovative, and have wide-ranging applications in a variety of areas. The world is currently afflicted by pollution, particularly water contamination, which is primarily caused by industrial effluents containing heavy metal ions and dyes. Furthermore, detecting viral pathogens that adhere to blood cells, gas leakage in industries is also a challenging issue for the human environment. It is an urgent requirement of a multifunctional material that will become a solution for all the above problems.

It is worthwhile paying attention to the carbon dots, which are a prominent and becoming emanate material in the classification of the carbon family. Because of the predominant luminescence property, biocompatibility, and comparatively low toxic and photostability, it has diverse applications in the field of sensing biomolecules, photocatalytic applications, chemiluminescence, electroluminescence sensing, mainly in the field of biosensors, optoelectronics, and also in the in-vitro and in-vivo imaging, drug-gene delivery system[1-4]. Carbon dots are an apparent subset of nanomaterial defined by the characteristics size less than 10 nm. Since the last decades, CDs have attracted researchers because of their unique characteristics and properties like outstanding solubility in water and low toxicity. It also has good photodynamic and photothermal stability. The preparation of carbon quantum dots using chemical synthesis increases the toxicity. Since it has good biosensing applications, best to synthesize using green synthesis by utilizing natural precursors, and the same can be easily achieved by the bottom-up approach than a top-down approach. The most simple, facile, and easiest economical method to synthesize carbon dots is the conventional hydrothermal method, otherwise called as the one-step reduction method. In this method, the toxicity of the material is under control which increases the biocompatibility [3,5]. Carbon dots are the best suitable material for sensing, which majorly depends upon the size of the particles. The fine-tuning of the size of the particles enhances the optical properties, and doping enhances the surface passivation, which increases the luminescence of the material. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (n-CDs) produces a high quantum field when compared to plain carbon dots. Because of the surface passivation, the atomic size and valence of the nitrogen, which is comparable with the carbon, results in the increment of enhancing the fluorescence property [6]. Avoiding environmentally harmful chemicals, which are also expensive, proceeding with green synthesis is the better method to synthesize carbon dots. Production of macroscale n-CDs on a large scale is still imperatively needed that, too by the easy way approach. Preparation of carbon dots can be done by employing citron fruits which usually depict a Quantum yield (QY) higher than the other natural precursors for a number of applications[7].

Industries' sewage contains Fe ions, which pollute the water. The detection of heavy metal ions in contaminated waterways is also crucial, that leads to many biological issues. At the fundamental building block level of the human system, Iron is the most basic constituent in the basic structure of haemoglobin, which plays a significant part in enzymic activities, and myoglobin plays a significant part in physiological and pathological actions involving oxygen transport and replacement. [8]. In neutral biological conditions, free Fe3+ will easily form Fe (OH)3, which is very tough to digest by the human system. This may also enhance the production of extremely reactive hydroxyl radicals, which result in the destroying of DNA, membrane, and as a chelating agent with the repressor proteins. This unbalancing of Fe leads to distinct disorders and diseases, such as anaemia and Alzheimer’s. Also, this could cause secondary infections and act as an inducer for fungal pathogens [9,10]. Although numerous publications exist in the field of heavy metal ion detection, highly selective and sensitive carbon dots are still required [11].

Gas sensor requires comparable care because it works with the environment, notably for the safety of humans. Several studies have found that diverse materials' ability to detect gases is related to their wide range of applications in industries, air quality monitoring, and health care facilities. Alcohol inhaling (ethanol) initially circumvents the first stage of metabolism and then quickly attains the bloodstream and nervous system, especially the brain. As a result, this causes addiction disorders and mental health diagnoses [12]. To prevent toxic gases from leaking and endangering the human body, various gases must be detected and examined. Ethanol is considered as one of the toxic gas which is used in the preparation of gasoline in fuel industries, alcoholic beverages industries, and used as solvents in paint industries. The development of nanostructural materials, nanoparticles, nanobelts, and nanowires has led to the conclusion that a smaller material size and high specific surface area would improve gas sensor sensitivity and response times. Carbon quantum dots, which have a zero-dimensional property with a sub-nanometer size and a high surface area, can detect the presence of ethanol in solution as well as verify the legitimacy of alcohol products.

A well-established gas sensing scheme is Scanning Kelvin Probe (SKP), where surface work function analysis is carried out in non destructive manner. This method is preferred to overwhelm the trouble of analyzing the low-level sensing signals [13]. For toxic gas sensing employing SKP, many semiconductors or metal oxide base quantum dots are accessible in research fields. As for our knowledge, this is the first work to show the characterization of carbon quantum dots with SKP for ethanol gas sensing. Using SKP, work function (ɸ) can be calculated, and sensing can be shown by variation in the work function.

Carbon dots have made significant contributions in the above-mentioned disciplines, such as metal ion detection, gas sensing, and fluorescent ink among others. Because of their amazing fluorescence qualities, the ink could be employed for information storage and sophisticated anti-counterfeiting [14,15]. Gravure printing also uses this type of CDs ink blended with certain resins [16].

In the present study, a cost-effective, locally available Citrus medica (C. Medica, Narthangai in Tamil Nadu) was used as the carbon precursor. It is rich in vitamin C used as a medicinal plant, as well as in preparing pickles. The synthesized n-CDs reveal an excellent fluorescence intensity. This can be modified by varying the temperature, time, and pH. n-CDs have become a popular sensor for detecting metal ions below the World Health Organisation acceptable limit, fluorescent ink, and gas leaks in industrial settings.

Citrus medica (C. Medica) fruit was acquired from Thiruvarur and Chennai, Tamilnadu, in South India. Aqueous ammonia (NH4OH 25%) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. The analytical grade of chemicals like CuCl2, Cd (CH3OO)2, ZnCl2, CaCl2, AlCl3, Fe3O4, and Fe2O3 have also been purchased from Avra synthesis, India. All the chemicals were used as it was received.

n-CDs were synthesized by using the conventional hydrothermal method. The procured C. Medica fruits were washed with Double Distilled Water (DDW), and the juice was squeezed by hand. The fresh juice was collected, and the same was disseminated through Whatman filter paper to obtain the juice without the silts and precipitate. Then 40 ml of the clear juice was moved to the Teflon coated stainless autoclave. Then it was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. in a vacuum oven. After the hydrothermal process at the required condition, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. A mixed cellulose acetate membrane filter was used for filtering the solution of carbon dots with a pore size of 0.22 μm. The derived carbon dots in the form of a solution were stored at 5 °C in a refrigerator for future use. The simple juice was severely acidic. Ammonia solution was added up to the juice and sonicated for dual purposes, one is for adjusting the pH value to neutral and basic and the other one is like a doping agent of nitrogen over the carbon dots. The experiments for the three pH values (1, 7, and 12) and three different temperatures (180, 210, and 240 °C) at two different timings (5 h, 8 h) were performed. The parameters were optimized to achieve the maximum fluorescence from the synthesized n-CDs with appreciable quantum yield. The identical plan of action was followed for various pH and different temperature-dependent experiments. In the supplementary material, a photographic picture representation of the work at each stage was shown in Figure S1. The figure depicted the synthesis process in detail.

To find out the chemical components, structural, elemental analysis, and optical properties of the n-CDs, a variety of physical-chemical characterizations techniques such as Ultraviolet–Visible(UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy (Model: Cary-60, Agilent technologies), Fluorescence spectroscopy(Model: JASCO, FP – 8200), Zeta potential(Make: Malvern PAN ANALYTICAL), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Make: PAN ANALYTICAL), Fourier Transform Infra-red spectroscopy (FT-IR, Make: Perkin Elmer), High-resolution transmittance electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Model: TECNAI, G2-F30ST WIN, USA), Energy dispersive spectroscopy (FESEM with EDS Make: FEI Quanta 200), X-ray photon electron spectroscopy (XPS, Make: ULVAC-PHI, Inc; Model: PHI5000 Version Probe III) Scanning Kelvin Probe Microscopy. (SKP, Model: SKP5050 + SPV, KP Technology Ltd, UK), and CH instruments (Model: CHI660E, Electrochemical workstation) were performed. In the supplementary material, the sample preparation for characterization was outlined.

Owing to examine the n-CDs optical properties, UV–Vis’s absorption spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy were taken at ambient temperature. The UV–Vis spectra of the as-synthesized carbon dots at various timings, pH, and different reaction temperatures were studied (refer to Fig. 1 .). The pH of fresh juice received from the citrus medica fruits is highly acidic (pH 1), and the same has undergone the hydrothermal synthesis showing an absorption peak at 271 nm assigned to the n-π* transitions of the C=O band, and due to the C=C bond,π-π* transition occurs. [17]. The absorbance curve of as-synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots at various times reveals that the absorbance is highest at 8 h and also in acidic conditions. Synthesized CDs at the acidic conditions are the plain CDs. It also shows the broad peak conversion at 340 nm due to the changes from the phenolate ions into ammonium phenolate ions [18]. Due to the π-π* transition, the main absorbance is observed at 271 nm associated with the sp2 electrons in the carbon matrix. [19]. After the doping process, another absorbance at 345 nm was clearly visible due to the n–π* transitions band, which was attributed to the increased amount of dopants in the carbon sp2 or sp3 matrix[20,21].

The value of absorbance decreases with an increase in pH and temperature. The broad absorption peak is observed at 340 nm due to surface functional groups, which could lead to strong fluorescence. For all n-CDs, Tauc plots were created and shown as an inset in the respective absorption spectra and the information gathered is summarised in the Table is shown in the supplementary material. Despite carbon core state variances, no major departures from the optical band gap are identified, demonstrating that the state responsible for emission is shared among all n-CDs. The bandgap fluctuates between 5.44 and 5.77 electron volts. The bandgap begins to narrow after doping, as seen in the Tauc plot [22].

The excitation wavelength and the emission spectra are shown in Fig. 2 . The utmost fluorescence intensity was achieved at the 310 nm excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission was obtained at 410 nm. The Measurement of the quantum yield of the material was calculated at 350 nm excitation by taking quinine sulfate as standard. The calculation was carried out by using the standard procedure. In brief, 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY is 0.54 at 350 nm) was taken as a value of standard reference.

The following equation (1) was used for calculating relative quantum yield.
(1)
QY
%
=QY
R
I
S
A
R
n
S
2
I
R
A
s
n
R
2

Where “I” denotes the integrated fluorescent emission intensity area of the emission curve at 350 nm excitation, A is the absorbance at 350 nm, “n” is the solvent refractive index. The subscript “R” and “S” represent the parameter of known reference (quinine sulphate) and the sample (n-CDs), respectively [23]. The quantum yield of the as-synthesized carbon dots was around 16 % measured at 350 nm excitation Wavelength. At pH 7 (neutral), it shows the maximum fluorescence intensity when compared to acidic and basic nature. In other words, the intensity increases from increasing pH from acidic to neutral and starts decreasing from neutral to basic as shown in Fig. 2(c). Similarly, it also exposes maximum fluorescence at 240 °C for 8 h, so the optimized condition for the applications is 240 °C for 8 h with pH 7. The emission spectra for an optimized condition at different excitation are also shown which clearly states that the maximum emission occurs at 410 nm wavelength when it is excited by 310 nm.

This shows decreasing nature after 310 nm excitation and also observed the shift towards longer wavelength (red shift) due to the surface passivation, presence of different sizes of the carbon dots, and also due to emissive trap sites on each n-CDs. The neutral pH was taken for further application proceedings like metal detection, ink, and gas sensing applications.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the n-CDs before and after a year of storage which exhibit, no major variations in the emission intensity both before and after one year of storage, are shown in the supplementary as Figure S2. The n-CDs solution was noticed with the naked eyes under UV light and remains with the same fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm in the UV cabinet. These results proved that the synthesized carbon dots have excellent prolong stability for a long time.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed in order to study the functional groups on the surface of the as-synthesized n-CDs. In Figure S3, the FT-IR spectrum clearly shows the presence of OH/NH stretching vibrations as a broadband peak in the range of 3375–3250 cm−1 [20,24,25]. The absorption bands at 2127 cm−1 are ascribed to the generation of the bonding of C≡C stretching. The sharp bands at 1638 cm−1 clearly state the C=C stretching and N-H bending vibrations, Stretching vibrations of C-N at 1388 cm−1 in the n-CDs [24,26,27]. The vibration modes of symmetric stretching of epoxy (C-O-C) groups are clearly stated at about 1035 and 1085 cm−1 [20,25]. On account of the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, n-CDs are hydrophilic and stable in polar solvents, which helps to proceed with biological applications.

Intending to learn the distinctive features of the surface and elemental composition of n-CDs, XPS characterization was carried out, which is presented in Fig. 3 . The XPS spectra reveal that the synthesized sample has an atomic percentage of carbon 74.2%, oxygen 17.3%, and nitrogen 8.5% (shown in the figure as inset). This reveals the confirmation of the successful inclusion of nitrogen with the carbon dots. The spectra of XPS show the peak at 285 eV for C 1s, 399 eV for N 1s, and 531 eV for O 1s [28]. The spectra of C 1s peak can be deconvoluted into three components at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, and 288 eV, which are assigned to the chemical environments of C=C/C-N, C-OH, C=O, respectively [29,30].

The deconvoluted spectra of N 1s exhibit components at 398.6 eV, 399.6, 400.2 and 401.3 eV corresponding to the pyridinic, amine, pyrrolic, quarternary graphitic nitrogen, respectively [31,32]. The spectra of O 1s exhibits a peak at 530.4 eV for HO-C=O, 531.2 eV for C-OH/C-O-C, 532.3 eV for C=O, and 533.7 eV for H-O-H [33,34]. Thus, our XPS results divulge that the material n-CDs are enriched with carbon and also with a considerable amount of nitrogen and oxygen. The different surface states of nitrogen combined with the sp2 carbon matrix enhances the optical properties. EDS analyses were also performed as part of the chemical composition study, and the results are reported in the supplementary material with Figure S4. XPS and FTIR shows clearly that nCDs are enriched by functional surface groups.

The result of HR-TEM is presented in Fig. 4 , clearly shows the crystallinity and surface morphology of the as-synthesized n-CDs. Fig. 4 (a) reveals the presence of carbon dots. Fig. 4(b) shows the separated carbon dots almost in a spherical shape. Fig. 4(c, d) shows the presence of lattice fringes with 0.31 nm of an interplanar distance at the (002) plane. Fig. 4(e) shows the SAED diffraction pattern image, which reveals the information that the as-synthesized carbon dots are near to polycrystalline nature or partially crystalline and also matches with the (002) plane spacing. The same was matched with the XRD results, which show a broad peak at 21.382° [35].

The size distribution histogram was plotted by doing statistical analysis using Image J software[36]. The average particle size was found as 4.5 nm and the average was calculated by counting 100 particles having the range from 2.5 nm to 6.3 nm. The interlayer spacing has been found as 0.31 nm, which is very closer to the graphite (0.34 nm) may exist as carbon dots with graphitic nature [37,38]. The XRD results also show additional peaks around 29°, which confirms the formation of graphitized carbon dots[33].

The zeta potential characterization was done to determine the surface electronic charge of the n-CDs. It shows a negative potential of −29.8 mV, which is shown in Figure S4(a). The negative ζ (Zeta) potential is because of the dense electron cloud concentrating on the CDs in the presence of functional groups like hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface [39]. The ζ value for Fe3+ coordinated n-CDs shows a positive potential around 24.2 mV in Figure S4. (b). This behavior is due to a mixture of oxygen-containing functional groups that form a compound with the Fe3+ ion. This results in the quenching of fluorescence (optical quenching), confirm the applications of sensing of ions particularly Fe3+ ions present in the wastewater and also in the biological applications where Fe3+ is the backbone for many enzymic activities. The compatibility of n-CDs with blood cells has been investigated for this purpose, and the results were reported in the supplementary material in Figure S8[40,41].

The work function measurement of n-CDs was studied through the SKP system at room temperature using a gold tip as a reference. A detailed working principle of the SKP system was reported in our previous paper [42-45]. SKP system was used to characterize the work function (ɸ) for carbon quantum dot in air and ethanol medium. The n-CDs solution was deposited on a cleaned FTO substrate (1 cm × 2 cm) by the drop-casting method. The raster scan 3D mapping for the n-CDs in air and ethanol medium is shown in Fig. 5 (a).

The raster scan image signifies the average work function for n-CDs in ambient conditions and ethanol gas medium is 4.95 eV and 5.08 eV under dark medium with a contact potential difference of 0.13 eV. This was due to the shift of the Fermi level towards the valence band since ethanolis a reducing gas. It is clear that theelectrons will be transferred after the ethanol gas is supplied. On the other hand, electrons in the conduction band will be captured by oxygen molecules adsorbed on the surface of the materials, resulting in the formation of oxygen ions and a rise in the Fermilevel. Work function of the material is defined as the energy required to set Fermi electron free, an increase in work function further corroborates synergy effect of carbon dots with VOC illustrated in 5(b). The following formula is used for calculating the work function,
(3)
Work f u n c t i o n
( ϕ )
=
ϕ
Au
-
CPD
Au
+
CPD
sample

where ɸAu is the work function of gold tip (5.1 eV),
CPD
Au
is the contact potential difference (CPD) measured between Au tip and Au coated plate and
CPD
sample
represents CPD measured between the tip and the sample.

The changes in the work function clearly reveal that carbon dots can be used as gas sensors for ethanol. Ethanol has both an accepting and donating nature which influences gas interaction and chemisorption towards the carbon quantum dots rich in OH functional groups[46]. Since carbon dots are enhanced by nitrogen doping, can offer more adsorption sites for sensing. This research contributes to a better understanding of the gas interaction properties of carbon dots which will aid in the development of new chemical sensor devices.This could be a wonderful material to use as a grove gas sensor in industries. Comparative analysis of gas sensing with other harmful gas are reported in the supplementary material figure S9.

As per earlier reports, the reason behind the quenching is a virtue of the electron transfer between the metal ions and CDs [47,48]. After being irradiated by the light the electron transfer to the receptors i.e metal ions results in the quenching. Up to the best of our erudition, this is the first work that exhibits the fluorescence quenching by n-CDs from C. Medica fruit towards Fe3+ ions.

To learn more about the electronic states indulged in the transition process, the following formula was used to calculate the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) energy levels[49]:
(4)
E
LUMO
= - e
E
red
+ 4.4
(5)
E
HOMO
= - e
E
ox
+ 4.4

The onset reduction and oxidation potentials (Ered and Eox) for n-CDs was measured in solution using cyclic voltammetry and Ered was found to be −0.33 eV. ELUMO was computed using the equation (4)
(6)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO -
E g

Eg was calculated from the absorption spectrum to be 5.57 eV for the optimised conditions. The calculated value of EHOMO is −9.64 eV. As shown in the Fig. 6 electrons in the LUMO levels (excited state) were transferred to the ‘d’ orbital of Fe3+ upon complex formation, resulting in optical quenching in view of that electron transfer in the radiation form was restricted.

To prove and identify the fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ions by as-prepared n-CDs, the optical quenching behavior was taken out for different ions with a mass of 1 mM at a wavelength of excitation 310 nm excitation wavelength. In Figure S7 the schematic of ion sensing behavior by n-CDs is shown. While comparing the results, it strongly suggests that the fluorescence of Fe3+ ions is highly quenched by the as-synthesized n-CDs than by other different metal ions like Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+ as proved by the emission spectra shown in Fig. 7 (a). This shows the strong association between n-CDs and Fe3+ metal ions. Metal ions are highly coordinating with the surface mainly functional groups like carboxyl, amino groups available in the surface, and the radiative transition was disturbed which results in the quenching. This further confirms that the as-synthesized sample shows high selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. Fig. 7(b) displays the bar diagram for (F0–F)/F0 where F represents the fluorescence intensity with various metal ions and fluorescence intensity without metal ions is denoted by F0. This graph reveals that the as-prepared n-CDs show highly appreciable selectivity towards Fe3+ as a fluorescent sensor. In order to show the sensitivity, the n-CDs solution was treated with various ranges of concentrations of Fe3+ from 2 μM to 2 mM. This clearly states that there is a continuous decrement of emission intensity which is distinctly shown by the photographic image in Fig. 8 (c). The above things obviously tell the electron affinity towards the Fe3+ ions and also another characteristic of the prepared n-CDs is that one behaves like electron donors as well as receptors.

Fig. 7(c) implies the sensitivity of n-CDs towards Fe3+ ions with increasing concentration from (0–20 μM) and the curtailment of intensity was displayed in the magnified inset. Fig. 7(d) employs the decrement performance of emission spectra of n-CDs. Fig. 8(a) shows the correlation between the intensity of fluorescence and the concentration of metal Fe3+ ions. The charge/electron transfer between both the n-CDs and Fe3+ ions results in the quenching of fluorescence. The inset in Fig. 8(a) shows the quenching by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the emission signal, a reasonable linear relation in lower concentration and distracted in the higher level. All the above information employs the metal ions might results in the formation of metal hydroxides because of the –OH functional groups present at the surface and on the edges of the n–CDs [7]. The (F0–F)/F0 value was used to determine the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of (0–25 µM), resulting in a satisfactory linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.998, as shown in Fig. 8(b). Based on equation (7), the LOD is calculated to be 1.2 µM.
(7)
L O D =
3 σ
s

where σ represents the standard deviation and s denotes the slope of the linear calibration. These results prove that n-CDs can be utilized for the selective quenching of Fe3+ present in wastewater, especially industrial effluents. According to the WHO report, the maximum permitted limit for Fe3+ in drinking water is 5.36 µM [50]. Our n-CDs are capable of detecting Fe3+ ions at levels below the legal limit. Table.1 shows a table of comparisons with other quantum dots for ion sensing.

Fluorescence ink is one of the most popular inks used for gravure printing applications as well as exclusively for paper, plastic, or deposited on glass. These n-CDs aqueous solutions could be directly utilized as ink by injecting without any modification. Writing on glass can be clearly seen with the aid of a UV cabinet and UV light of 365 nm. In ambient light, n-CDs aqueous solutions appear brown, but under UV light, they show a dazzling blue fluorescence. n-CDs ink solution is fair, everlasting, deterioration-free, excellent dispersibility, and easily erasable [58]. This could be a substitute source for fluorescent pens. The photographic image for the fluorescent ink application is presented in Fig. 9 .

In essence, for the first time, the lustered n-CDs were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal method, from Citrus medica and aqueous ammonia as the precursors of carbon and nitrogen respectively. Doping of nitrogen was strongly done into the structure of n-CDs which was confirmed by the XPS, EDS and FT-IR spectra analyses result in glowing n-CDs. These carbon dots have an average diameter of approximately 4.5 nm. This also revealed an outstanding solubility in water, emission behavior is purely excitation-dependent with prolonged stability, and strong fluorescence emission under various pH, temperature, and timings. n-CDs also revealed the fluorescence quantum yield of 16.3% and the same can be applied as the fluorescent probe sensor via induced electron transfer for the heavy metal ion sensing with LOD of 1.2 µM from industrial wastewater with high selectivity and sensitivity. The rich surface OH functional groups that enhance electron interaction with the ethanol gas medium have been clearly demonstrated by CPD mapping with SKP. It has a wide range of applications in gas sensing in industries. Without any modification, this can be used as fluorescent inks. This ink is pollution-free, permanent, easily washable, and free to flow without any clotting. n-CDs are biocompatible with the red blood cells. Based on the experimental findings, we trust that synthesized n-CDs could be a potential candidate for a variety of detection applications and developments. This research will help to promote and develop a method for sensing toxic gas utilizing carbon quantum dots and SKP.

Nithya Selvaraju: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing–original draft. Senthilnathan Selvaraj: Validation. Neeraj Singhal: Investigation. Vigneshwaran Mohan: Data curation. Yuvaraj Sivalingam: Resources. Kaushik Rajaram: Resources. Gunasekaran Venugopal: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing–review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are very thankful to the Central University of Tamil Nadu, India for providing an infrastructural, institutional; research facility for this project and A special thanks to Mr. Nashath, Research scholar (Department of Microbiology, Central University of Tamil Nadu), who assisted with the biocompatibility test with RBC cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research Data Policy and Data Availability Statements
ce:section.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analysed in the current study, are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic representation, detailed sample preparation procedures for characterisation, summarised table of band gap, PL spectra for prolong stability, FTIR, EDS spectra, Zetapotential, fluroscent images, and hemolysis assay are shown. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2022.115792.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
